correctness.rationale: Constraints, FKs, and ADR-0001 triggers are generally sound; a few RLS scope and uniqueness/indexing gaps merit fixes but no clear P0s surfaced.

robustness.rationale: The ADR-0001 design centralizes attribution logic well; some trigger sprawl and duplicated view patterns create maintenance risk.

scalability.rationale: Indexes generally match query paths at current scale; a few targeted partial/composite indexes would future‑proof hot joins and LATERAL tiebreaks.

idiomaticness.rationale: Good use of DEFERRABLE FKs, GIN/HNSW, partial unique indexes; RLS policy targeting of public and duplicate single‑column indexes are the main non‑idiomatic spots.

response.summary:
Overall this is a solid, thoughtfully designed single‑user schema. The ADR‑0001 attribution is implemented cleanly: a single pure function derives the onyx_* dates, and per‑table BEFORE triggers supply the right source signals. Key parent/child relationships are now FK‑enforced (WHOOP cycle hub, Garmin laps→activities, Spotify plays→tracks/artists, supplement intake→products), and JSONB is used judiciously where schemas are open‑ended (supplement ingredients, journal topics, raw Spotify payloads). Views cover the primary analytics surfaces, with behavioral attribution now canonical via daily_health_matrix_behavioral.

What isn’t perfect: a handful of RLS policies grant ALL to public instead of service_role (harmless today due to missing table‑level write grants, but non‑idiomatic and risky for future grants). The HRV predictions table in production still shows the legacy composite PK, diverging from the intended surrogate‑PK + 4‑tuple UNIQUE design; adding that uniqueness and a supporting index will harden the latest/tiebreak views. The 135‑column behavioral matrix repeats three similar LATERAL‑LIMIT‑1 dedup blocks, increasing maintenance friction; factoring those into helper views (or materialized helper views) will reduce drift and improve explainability. Several duplicate single‑column indexes mirror PKs and can be dropped.

Riskiest design choices: reliance on role public in write‑policies, divergence between intended and live HRV predictions uniqueness, and the growing maintenance burden in the matrix view. If I could only fix three things: (1) normalize RLS so ALL policies target service_role, (2) apply the surrogate PK + UNIQUE quad and add a covering index to support hrv_predictions_latest, and (3) extract the repeated LATERAL dedups into small helper views with appropriate indexes.

findings.title: RLS 'service_full_access' granted to public instead of service_role
findings.description: Several tables grant ALL to the public role rather than to service_role. It’s harmless today (no table-level INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE grants to anon/authenticated), but it widens the trust boundary and could become a write hole if future grants land.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: “Several (whoop_*, garmin_*, sync_log) target {public} instead… granting ALL to public is semantically broader than service_role.”
findings.recommendation: Replace ALL-to-public policies with ALL-to-service_role on affected tables; explicitly REVOKE ALL from public to avoid surprises.

findings.title: Missing explicit service write policies on some RLS-enabled tables
findings.description: A few tables have only anon_read and rely on Supabase's bypass-RLS for service writes. Works on Supabase but reduces portability and makes intent unclear.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: “A few tables have RLS enabled with anon_read but no service_* policy… rely on service-role's bypass-RLS privilege.”
findings.recommendation: Add a consistent service_full_access policy (TO service_role USING true WITH CHECK true) for all writeable tables.

findings.title: HRV predictions: intended surrogate PK + UNIQUE quad not reflected in prod
findings.description: Production shows PK (prediction_date, model, horizon_days), but intended DDL adds a surrogate id and UNIQUE(prediction_date, model, horizon_days, model_version) NULLS NOT DISTINCT. Without it, duplicates per model_version aren’t constrained and DISTINCT ON views rely on timing rather than integrity.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: PK (prediction_date, model, horizon_days). sql/hrv_predictions_surrogate_pk.sql: drops composite PK, adds id PK + UNIQUE quad.
findings.recommendation: Apply sql/hrv_predictions_surrogate_pk.sql to production; then add an index on (prediction_date, model, horizon_days, model_version, created_at DESC) to support hrv_predictions_latest’s tiebreak efficiently.

findings.title: Add covering/partial indexes for common SCORED/primary-branch joins
findings.description: Hot joins repeatedly filter by score_state='SCORED' and is_nap=false, and LATERAL blocks ORDER BY overall_sleep_score LIMIT 1. Current single-column indexes help but don’t exploit these predicates.
findings.evidence: daily views join whoop_recovery on (cycle_id AND score_state='SCORED') and whoop_sleep on (cycle_id AND is_nap=false AND score_state='SCORED'); garmin_sleep LATERAL orders by overall_sleep_score DESC LIMIT 1.
findings.recommendation: Create partial indexes: whoop_recovery(cycle_id) WHERE score_state='SCORED'; whoop_sleep(cycle_id) WHERE score_state='SCORED' AND is_nap=false; a composite on garmin_sleep(onyx_behavioral_date, overall_sleep_score DESC) WHERE is_nap=false AND sleep_id IS NOT NULL.

findings.title: Matrix view repeats LATERAL+LIMIT-1 patterns; factor into helper views
findings.description: garmin_sleep best-of-day, garmin_hrv latest-of-day, and whoop_cycles longest-of-day are repeated LATERAL dedups in large views. This increases drift risk and hinders reuse.
findings.evidence: Multiple LEFT JOIN LATERAL (...) LIMIT 1 blocks in daily_health_matrix_behavioral and its main_session variant.
findings.recommendation: Create small helper views (e.g., garmin_sleep_best_per_behavioral_date, garmin_hrv_latest_per_behavioral_date, whoop_main_cycle_per_behavioral_date) with the same dedup logic and appropriate indexes; join those from both matrix views.

findings.title: Legacy and behavioral matrix views coexist; deprecate the legacy one
findings.description: Two parallel matrix views invite accidental use of the wrong attribution regime. A legacy snapshot already exists for rollback/A‑B.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: both views present; sql/adr_0001_07_snapshot_daily_health_matrix.sql snapshots the legacy.
findings.recommendation: Move pds.daily_health_matrix to pds_legacy.daily_health_matrix_legacy (or drop with clear migration notes) and update consumers to the behavioral view.

findings.title: Duplicate single-column indexes that duplicate PKs
findings.description: These indexes duplicate the leading key(s) of existing PK/UNIQUE constraints and add write overhead without improving plans.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md lists: myfitnesspal_nutrition PK(calendar_date) + idx(calendar_date); weight_log PK(log_date) + idx(log_date DESC); whoop_journal PK(cycle_date, question) + idx(cycle_date).
findings.recommendation: Drop the redundant single‑column indexes; keep the PK/UNIQUE only.

findings.title: Free-text enums lack CHECKs (bed_side, score_state, mood, kind)
findings.description: Several categorical fields are TEXT with de facto enumerations. Typos would silently create new categories and break filters.
findings.evidence: eight_sleep_trends.bed_side comment indicates 'left'/'right'; whoop tables use SCORED; meal_events.kind lists nominal values; journal_entries.mood has a small set.
findings.recommendation: Add CHECK constraints or enum types: bed_side IN ('left','right'); whoop score_state IN ('SCORED','PENDING_SCORE','UNSCORABLE'); meal_events.kind IN (...); journal_entries.mood IN (...).

findings.title: Habit/Whoop journal questions are free-text without a dim map
findings.description: Long-format journal stores (date, question) with free-text question values. Without a FK/dim, spelling drift or renames can fragment signals.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: “habit_journal.question ↔ habit_name_map.name — loose join in app code; no FK.”
findings.recommendation: Introduce a journal_questions dim (id, canonical_name, source) and store question_id in fact tables; enforce via FK; provide a mapping/backfill script.

findings.title: Trigger sprawl for ADR-0001 attribution increases drift surface
findings.description: 11+ nearly identical set_onyx_dates_* triggers each call derive_onyx_dates with minor variations, making it easy for behavior to drift across tables.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: “11 of these… each calling pds.derive_onyx_dates().”
findings.recommendation: Refactor to a single parameterized trigger function (table/column config via TG_ARGV) or consolidate shared logic; add lightweight regression tests to validate per-table semantics.

findings.title: Behavioral matrix still emits onyx_* as calendar_date literals
findings.description: The view sets onyx_behavioral_date/onyx_et_date/onyx_local_date to the same s.calendar_date literal. It’s harmless but semantically odd alongside ADR-0001: consumers may assume these were recomputed per-source.
findings.evidence: SELECT list: s.calendar_date AS onyx_behavioral_date, s.calendar_date AS onyx_et_date, s.calendar_date AS onyx_local_date.
findings.recommendation: Either drop these passthrough aliases from the view or rename them to avoid confusion (or add a comment in the view definition clarifying they’re the spine’s behavioral date).

findings.title: Consider indexing spotify_daily_signature inputs for future growth
findings.description: The daily signature aggregates by played_date_et with a LEFT JOIN to tracks; current indexes cover played_date_et and track_id, which is fine now but add a partial index to keep scans tight as plays grow.
findings.recommendation: Add a multicolumn index on spotify_plays(played_date_et, track_id) to speed GROUP BY + join, and consider a partial index on spotify_tracks(track_id) WHERE valence IS NOT NULL to favor featurized rows.

things_done_well.title: ADR‑0001 centralization with derive_onyx_dates + triggers
things_done_well.why_it_matters: A pure, reusable function plus lightweight per‑table triggers ensures consistent behavioral‑day attribution and catches TZ edge cases at write time.

things_done_well.title: Use of DEFERRABLE FKs on Spotify ingest
things_done_well.why_it_matters: DEFERRABLE INITIALLY DEFERRED avoids fragile ingest ordering while preserving referential integrity — idiomatic for ETL pipelines.

things_done_well.title: JSONB where it belongs, with indexes
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Open‑ended payloads with GIN indexing allow flexible queries without premature schema lock‑in; promotion to columns is used only where the shape is stable.

things_done_well.title: Partial unique index to enforce one open habit interval
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Enforcing a single open interval in history tables prevents subtle logic bugs and is a clean, idiomatic Postgres pattern.

things_done_well.title: Safety/diagnostic views for pipeline health
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Proactive canaries surface missing predictions and TZ‑log drift early, reducing silent analytic errors.

questions_for_followup: Can we list the exact tables whose service_full_access policies currently target public so we can batch‑fix them with one migration?
Are any write privileges (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) granted to authenticated or anon outside this bundle (e.g., via Supabase dashboard)?
Do consumers of hrv_predictions_latest ever filter by model_version explicitly, or should we add a filtered view per live/backtest to simplify usage?
Is whoop_workouts ever joined to a specific cycle_id in analytics (beyond date attribution)? If yes, should we add a derived cycle_id column to enable future FK?
Should habit/Whoop journal questions be normalized now (dim table) or do we prefer post‑hoc mapping in Python?
Is Eight Sleep ever used for the right bed side? If not, can we enforce bed_side='left' via CHECK or default to reduce accidental drift?
Can we safely drop the redundant single‑column indexes now, or do any client apps rely on their names for introspection?
